original_hypothesis.title: Unbounded Validator List Growth via _tryPushValidator Causes totalAssets DoS
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Every call to delegateAmount and redelegateAmount invokes _tryPushValidator for destination validators, adding new entries to the validators array. The array is only shrinkable via pruneValidatorList() which requires validators to have zero stake AND zero rewards across all proxies. Since totalAssets() iterates all validators for every proxy (O(validators * proxies) external calls to Ronin staking), unbounded growth can cause totalAssets() to exceed gas limits. This would DoS all deposit, withdraw, redeem, and requestWithdrawal operations since they all depend on totalAssets().
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: delegateAmount and redelegateAmount add validators but never remove source validators. Pruning requires explicit external call and may itself fail with large lists. The deployment script already delegates to 5 validators across 2 proxies, establishing the pattern.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _tryPushValidator unconditionally adds to validators array with no upper bound; validators.push(_validator) grows array indefinitely

preconditions: Operator adds many distinct validator addresses over time
Pruning is not performed regularly or becomes too expensive
Multiple proxies amplify gas cost per validator

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: totalAssets cost ~= validators.length * proxies * external_call_gas. With 200 validators and 10 proxies, ~2000 external calls per totalAssets invocation
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Gradual validator accumulation or operator adding many validators in redelegation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Protocol-wide DoS: all user operations blocked when totalAssets exceeds block gas limit

economic_questions: What is the gas cost per validator per proxy in getTotalStaked/getTotalRewards?
At what validator count does totalAssets exceed block gas limit?
Can pruneValidatorList itself be DoS'd at high validator counts?
validation_requirements: Benchmark gas cost of totalAssets with increasing validator counts
Determine Ronin chain block gas limit
Test pruneValidatorList gas consumption at scale

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; protocol DoS as griefing

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Slow accumulation; trigger point when gas limit exceeded
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete protocol freeze for all users

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Cannot deposit, withdraw, or redeem; funds locked

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Current validator count (5 in deployment), proxy count (2), Ronin gas limit
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Gas profiling of totalAssets at various validator counts

validation_result.mechanism: Unbounded array growth with self-DoS-able pruning
validation_result.impact_summary: The validators array in ValidatorTracker has no upper bound, and the pruneValidatorList() function itself has O(validators * proxies) gas cost, meaning it can become too expensive to prune before totalAssets() becomes too expensive to call, eventually bricking all deposit/withdraw/redeem operations.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _tryPushValidator function (ValidatorTracker.sol:27-33) adds validators to an unbounded array. The only removal mechanism is pruneValidatorList() which iterates all validators and makes O(validators * proxies) external calls (getReward per validator per proxy, plus getManyStakingAmounts per validator). totalAssets() calls getTotalStaked() and getTotalRewards(), each of which passes the full validators array to external calls per proxy. While the number of external calls is O(2 * proxies), the gas cost per call is proportional to validators.length due to calldata encoding, external contract processing, and return data iteration. The critical defect is that pruning is itself vulnerable to the same gas scaling issue - if the validators array grows large enough that pruneValidatorList() exceeds the gas limit, the array can never be shrunk, creating a permanent DoS. The attack requires operator cooperation (only onlyOperator functions call _tryPushValidator), limiting attack realism, but the code defect of missing an upper bound is genuine. Over the operational lifetime of the protocol with validator rotation, the list can grow monotonically even with legitimate operations.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: ValidatorTracker.sol:27-33 shows _tryPushValidator with no max length check. LiquidRon.sol:155-169 (delegateAmount) and LiquidRon.sol:176-186 (redelegateAmount) call _tryPushValidator for every destination validator. LiquidRon.sol:243-246 (totalAssets) calls getTotalStaked and getTotalRewards, which iterate the full validator list per proxy. LiquidRon.sol:192-215 (pruneValidatorList) has O(validators * proxies) external calls, making the pruning mechanism itself susceptible to gas limits at scale.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No upper bound check on validators array length. Each unique validator address added grows the array permanently until pruned, but pruning itself has O(validators * proxies) gas cost.

Pruning makes O(validators * proxies) external calls to getReward plus O(validators) calls to getManyStakingAmounts. If the validators array grows large, pruning itself exceeds gas limits, creating an irrecoverable state.
Calls getTotalStaked and getTotalRewards which pass the full validators array to external calls per proxy. Gas cost grows with validators.length * proxyCount.

calling_sequence: 1. Operator calls delegateAmount with new consensus addresses
2. _tryPushValidator called for each address (ValidatorTracker.sol:27-33), adding to validators[]
3. Over time, many unique validators accumulate in the array
4. User calls deposit() which internally calls totalAssets() (LiquidRon.sol:243-246)
5. totalAssets calls getTotalStaked() (LiquidRon.sol:219-227) iterating all validators per proxy
6. totalAssets calls getTotalRewards() (LiquidRon.sol:231-239) iterating all validators per proxy
7. With sufficient validators and proxies, gas exceeds block limit
8. pruneValidatorList() (LiquidRon.sol:192-215) also exceeds gas limit, preventing recovery
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is: _tryPushValidator adds validators → totalAssets iterates all validators via _getTotalStakedInProxy and _getTotalRewardsInProxy → each makes external calls with arrays proportional to validators.length → pruneValidatorList has O(validators * proxies) external calls (getReward individually per validator per proxy at lines 204-206) making it even more expensive than totalAssets.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Deployment uses 5 validators and 2 proxies as initial state
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script shows 5 consensus addresses delegated across 2 proxies. This establishes the pattern but at low scale. The defect manifests over operational lifetime as new validators are added.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack requires accumulation of unique validator addresses over time through legitimate or semi-legitimate operator actions (delegateAmount/redelegateAmount to new validators). On Ronin, the validator set is finite but can grow with network evolution. The pruneValidatorList function's O(validators * proxies) external call pattern (individual getReward calls at LiquidRon.sol:205) means pruning becomes more expensive than totalAssets faster, creating a trap where recovery is impossible once a threshold is reached. While the immediate practical risk is limited by Ronin's current validator count (~22-50), the code has no guardrails against future growth.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If the validators array grows large enough, totalAssets() exceeds the block gas limit, which DoS's all deposit(), withdraw(), redeem(), requestWithdrawal(), and finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() operations since they all depend on share price calculations. The pruneValidatorList() function hits gas limits first due to its higher per-validator gas cost (individual getReward calls vs batched calls), preventing recovery. All user funds would be locked.
recommended_mitigations: Add a MAX_VALIDATORS constant and check in _tryPushValidator: require(validatorCount < MAX_VALIDATORS)
Allow pruning in batches by accepting start/end indices instead of iterating the full list
Consider allowing the operator/owner to force-remove specific validators
Add a paginated version of totalAssets or cache staking totals to avoid recalculating every call
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LiquidRon with 2 proxies. 2. As operator, call delegateAmount repeatedly with unique validator addresses (use mock staking contract). 3. Measure gas cost of totalAssets() at 10, 50, 100, 200 validators. 4. Measure gas cost of pruneValidatorList() at same thresholds. 5. Identify the validator count at which each exceeds Ronin block gas limit. 6. Verify pruneValidatorList fails at a lower threshold than totalAssets due to individual getReward calls.
uncertainties: Ronin chain's exact block gas limit (different from Ethereum mainnet)
Gas cost of IRoninValidator.getReward, getRewards, and getManyStakingAmounts external calls on Ronin
Practical number of unique validators that would be used over the protocol's lifetime
Whether the onlyOperator bug (inverted logic) would prevent the operator from ever adding validators in practice
additional_testing_needed: Gas profiling of totalAssets() and pruneValidatorList() with increasing validator counts on a Ronin fork
Determine Ronin's block gas limit and calculate maximum safe validator count
Test whether pruneValidatorList hits gas limit before totalAssets does
Verify behavior when pruning partially succeeds (some validators removed before gas runs out)
